parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I was just sitting on the couch trying to unwind when my chest suddenly tightened and my breathing got shallow. It caught me completely off guard, even though I knew there was nothing actually wrong in the room. I have been noticing these little spikes lately, usually when I finally try to let my guard down. It is frustrating because I just want to relax, but my body keeps acting like it is waiting for something bad to happen. I end up pacing around the living room until the feeling slowly fades away.
turns.model_response:
I am sorry you’re going through this. It is incredibly draining when the time you’ve set aside for peace becomes the time your body decides to go on high alert.

What you’re describing—that spike of anxiety right when you try to "let your guard down"—is actually a very common, albeit frustrating, phenomenon. Sometimes, when we are busy or stressed during the day, our adrenaline keeps us pushed forward. It’s only when we finally sit still and the "noise" of the day stops that our nervous system realizes it has a backlog of stress to process. It’s like your body has been holding its breath all day and finally lets out a gasp, but it feels like a panic attack instead.

It makes total sense that you end up pacing. Your brain has triggered a "fight or flight" response, and pacing is your body’s way of trying to "do" something with all that sudden, misplaced energy.

Since this has been happening more lately, have you found anything that helps besides pacing? Sometimes, instead of fighting the feeling (which can make the chest tightness worse), it helps to acknowledge it out loud: *"My body feels unsafe right now, but I am actually safe in this room."*

Are there specific thoughts that come up when this happens, or does it feel purely physical, like a glitch in the system?

turns.user_message: That same tight feeling keeps showing up now, and it is starting to feel like a regular part of my routine. I will be doing something completely ordinary, like reading or making a snack, and suddenly my pulse races for no clear reason. It is exhausting trying to ignore it while I wait for the wave to pass on its own. I catch myself holding my breath and checking the time, wondering how long this round will last. The anticipation alone makes it harder to settle back into whatever I was doing before it hit.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be in a constant state of "waiting for the other shoe to drop." When that physical tightness and racing pulse show up during ordinary moments, it robs you of the simple peace of just making a snack or reading a book. It makes sense that you feel exhausted; your body is essentially stuck in a high-alert survival mode while your mind is just trying to live your life.

The anticipation you mentioned—that "checking the time" and wondering when it will end—is often just as taxing as the physical symptoms themselves. It creates a loop where you’re not just experiencing the wave, but you’re also hyper-vigilant, watching for the next one.

Since this is becoming a regular part of your routine, have you had a chance to mention these physical symptoms to a doctor? Sometimes getting a professional to rule out or address the physical side of things can help lower the volume on that "anticipation" anxiety.

In the meantime, when you catch yourself holding your breath, try to remember that your body is trying to protect you from a threat that isn't there. Instead of trying to ignore it (which is so hard to do), some people find it helpful to acknowledge it: *"My heart is racing right now, and it’s uncomfortable, but I am safe."*

Does it feel like there’s a specific time of day when this happens most, or does it feel completely random?

turns.user_message: Yesterday it hit me so hard that I actually had to sit on the floor just to catch my breath. My whole body felt completely wired and shaky, like I had just sprinted a mile, even though I had not moved from the couch. I tried to focus on the wall in front of me, but my thoughts kept spinning into this frantic loop. Every little sound in the house felt amplified, and I could not shake the heavy sense that something was terribly wrong. It took forever for my heart to finally slow down, and I am just drained from carrying this constant weight.
turns.model_response:
I am so sorry you went through that. What you’re describing sounds incredibly intense and physically exhausting. That feeling of being "wired" while sitting still—where your body is in full fight-or-flight mode even though there is no immediate danger—is a heavy, draining experience.

It makes complete sense that you feel depleted today. When your nervous system hits a peak like that, it uses up an immense amount of energy. It’s like your body ran a marathon while you were sitting on the floor, and the "hangover" from that surge of adrenaline can leave you feeling heavy and hollow.

Please try to be very gentle with yourself today. When you’ve been through a spike of anxiety or a panic attack that severe, your body needs time to recalibrate. 

If you feel up to it, it might help to focus on very small, sensory comforts to help ground your nervous system:
*   **Temperature:** A cold cloth on your neck or a warm cup of tea can sometimes help signal to your brain that you are safe in the present moment.
*   **Low Stimulation:** Since sounds felt amplified yesterday, giving yourself some "quiet time" with low lights might help ease that sensory overload.
*   **Hydration:** These episodes can actually dehydrate you because of the physical stress.

Are you feeling any better physically today, or does that "heavy weight" still feel just as pressing?
